movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Controlled articular rotations for shoulder health

movements.name: Easy Run
movements.notes: Conversational pace to elevate heart rate

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Activate posterior chain and cardiovascular system

movements.name: Row Intervals
movements.notes: Target consistent split times. Focus on powerful leg drive and full recovery between efforts.

movements.name: Assault Bike
movements.notes: Moderate steady pace, avoid going to failure

movements.name: Pull-Up

metcon.target_score: Complete as many rounds as possible. Target 6-8 rounds for trained athletes.

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch
movements.notes: 90/90 position, focus on anterior hip opening

movements.name: Foam Roll - Quads
movements.notes: Slow passes, pause on tender areas

session_why.text: This session targets the oxidative energy system through sustained aerobic intervals and a long AMRAP. The rowing intervals develop aerobic power at sub-maximal intensity, while the 20-minute AMRAP maintains heart rate in the aerobic zone (65-75% max HR) to improve cardiovascular endurance. Gastin (2001) identifies the oxidative system as dominant beyond 3-4 minutes of effort, making this time domain ideal for aerobic base development.

movement_why.text: Monostructural movements (row, run, bike) are selected to minimize technical failure and keep intensity in the aerobic zone. Pull-ups are included in small sets to maintain work capacity without creating excessive muscular fatigue. This mixed-modal approach develops general physical preparedness across Glassman's cardiovascular endurance and stamina domains while preventing the monotony of single-modality cardio.

loading_why.text: The 500m row intervals at 70-75% effort allow sufficient recovery (90 seconds) to repeat quality efforts without entering the glycolytic system. The 20-minute AMRAP is designed for sustainable pacing - athletes should never reach failure on any movement. This polarized approach (80% of volume at low intensity) aligns with concurrent training principles to avoid interference with strength adaptations.
loading_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
loading_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movements.name: Thoracic Rotation
movements.notes: Improve thoracic spine mobility for overhead position

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch
movements.notes: Open anterior hip for better squat mechanics

movements.name: Thruster (Front Squat + Push Press)
movements.notes: Build to a moderate load that can be moved with speed. Focus on explosive hip drive from bottom of squat into overhead press. This is technique work at moderate intensity, not max effort.

movements.name: Thruster
movements.notes: Lighter load than strength block. Manage sets to avoid complete failure.

movements.name: Assault Bike
movements.notes: High effort, maintain consistent pace across rounds

metcon.target_score: Target time: 10-13 minutes. This should be sustainable high effort, not all-out sprint.

movements.name: Assault Bike Easy
movements.notes: Very low effort, active recovery pace

movements.name: Thoracic Rotation
movements.notes: Release thoracic tension from overhead work

session_why.text: This session targets the glycolytic energy system through repeated high-intensity efforts in the 10-15 minute time domain. The thruster-bike couplet creates sustained muscular and cardiovascular demand, training the body's ability to clear lactate and maintain power output under fatigue. The medium time domain (7-15 min) ensures glycolytic system dominance while avoiding excessive volume that would interfere with strength adaptation.

movement_why.text: Thrusters combine squat and vertical push patterns, creating high metabolic demand through large muscle recruitment. The assault bike provides additional cardiovascular stress without technical complexity. This pairing develops stamina - the ability to process, deliver, store, and use energy - across Glassman's ten physical skills framework.

loading_why.text: The E2MOM strength format at 70% 1RM builds technique and speed at moderate loads, exploiting the 2-minute interval for phosphocreatine recovery between sets. The metcon uses 50% of this load to allow sustainable effort across 5 rounds without technical breakdown. The 1:3 work:rest ratio within each round (thruster sets + bike) aligns with glycolytic system recovery demands.

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Establish rowing mechanics at low intensity

movements.name: Kettlebell Swing (Russian)
movements.notes: Focus on powerful hip extension, swing to eye level. This builds posterior chain endurance and power output. Maintain crisp hip snap on every rep.

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Moderate steady pace, focus on efficient stroke rate

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.notes: Small sets to preserve shoulder health and maintain aerobic pace

metcon.target_score: Target 7-9 rounds. Pace should be consistent across all rounds - this is aerobic capacity work, not max effort sprinting.

session_why.text: This session develops oxidative capacity through an 18-minute mixed-modal AMRAP that maintains heart rate in the aerobic zone. The combination of rowing, kettlebell swings, and pull-ups creates sustained cardiovascular demand while distributing muscular load across multiple movement patterns. The time domain (15-20 min) places primary stress on the oxidative energy system as identified by Gastin (2001).

movement_why.text: The movement selection balances monostructural cardio (row) with functional weightlifting (KB swing) and gymnastics (pull-up), embodying CrossFit's mixed-modal approach to GPP. The posterior chain emphasis (rowing, swings) develops the hinge pattern under sustained volume, while pull-ups maintain pulling strength capacity. This variety prevents local muscular failure while sustaining cardiovascular stress.

loading_why.text: The strength block uses straight sets at moderate load (5x20) to build kettlebell swing volume and work capacity. The metcon uses the same load but with American swings (overhead) and lower reps per round to manage fatigue across the 18-minute time domain. This concurrent approach - volume work followed by sustained conditioning - minimizes interference between strength and endurance adaptations when kept at moderate intensity.
loading_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
loading_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Shoulder preparation for ski erg pulling

movements.name: Ski Erg
movements.notes: Establish pulling mechanics at low intensity

movements.name: Box Jump (low box)
movements.notes: Plyometric primer, focus on soft landing

movements.name: Ski Erg Intervals
movements.notes: Each interval should be near-maximal effort. Focus on powerful arm pull and full hip hinge. This develops anaerobic capacity and upper body pulling power.

movements.name: Box Jump
movements.notes: Minute 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, 16: Fast reps, step down for safety

movements.name: Ski Erg
movements.notes: Minute 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18: Moderate-high effort, finish with time to spare

metcon.target_score: Complete all reps within each minute. Rest is the remainder of the minute. Target 15-30 seconds rest per movement.

movements.name: Foam Roll - Lats
movements.notes: Release lat tension from ski erg

session_why.text: This session targets the glycolytic energy system through high-intensity intervals in the strength block and sustained power output in the EMOM metcon. The ski erg intervals (200m at 85-90% effort) create repeated bouts of anaerobic work with incomplete recovery, training lactate clearance capacity. The EMOM format maintains elevated heart rate while allowing brief recovery between movements, sustaining effort in the 10-20 minute glycolytic-dominant time domain.
session_why.url_or_ref: Ch. 3 - Energy System Interaction and Exercise

movement_why.text: The movement selection emphasizes power development across multiple modalities. Box jumps develop lower body explosive power, double-unders train speed and coordination under fatigue, and ski erg intervals build upper body pulling power and cardiovascular capacity. This combination addresses Glassman's power (maximum force in minimum time) and speed (minimizing cycle time) physical skills while maintaining cardiovascular stress.

loading_why.text: The 200m ski erg intervals with 90-second rest create a 1:1 to 1:2 work:rest ratio appropriate for glycolytic system training. The EMOM metcon uses 60-second intervals to auto-regulate intensity - athletes must complete work efficiently to earn rest. This format prevents sandbagging while ensuring adequate recovery to maintain power output across all 18 minutes. The progression from high-intensity intervals to sustained EMOM work follows sound session sequencing.

movements.name: Sumo Deadlift High Pull
movements.load: Build from 45 lbs to 95/65 lbs across sets
movements.notes: Ramp/buildup progression scheme. Start light to establish mechanics: wide stance, vertical torso, elbows high. Build to a moderate working weight that allows crisp hip extension and high pull on every rep. This is not max effort - focus on speed and technique.

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Maintain strong pace, match bike effort

movements.name: Sumo Deadlift High Pull
movements.notes: Unbroken or quick singles, lighter than strength block load

metcon.target_score: Target time: 12-15 minutes. This should be sustained high effort with brief transitions between movements.

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch
movements.notes: Release hip flexors from cycling volume

session_why.text: This session develops mixed oxidative-glycolytic capacity through sustained monostructural work and moderate-load SDHP. The 12-16 minute For Time format creates continuous cardiovascular demand while the movement rotation prevents local muscular failure. The combination of bike and row cardio with the hinge+pull pattern (SDHP) trains the body's ability to sustain power output across multiple energy pathways, characteristic of the medium time domain (7-15 min).

movement_why.text: The monostructural cardio pieces (bike, row) provide high cardiovascular stress without technical complexity, allowing athletes to focus on output rather than movement quality. SDHP adds a functional weightlifting component that develops the posterior chain under fatigue. This mixed-modal approach embodies CrossFit GPP principles while keeping technical demand manageable under cardiovascular stress.

loading_why.text: The ramp/buildup progression scheme in the strength block (8-8-8-6-6 building load) provides neural preparation and technical refinement before the metcon. Starting light allows perfect mechanics; building across sets prepares the pattern under increasing load. Zatsiorsky & Kraemer identify progressive warm-up sets as essential for neural preparation and technique refinement. The metcon uses lighter load (75/55 vs 95/65) to maintain movement speed and prevent technical breakdown across 4 rounds.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training

movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Shoulder preparation for rowing and pull-ups

movements.name: Row Intervals
movements.notes: Maintain consistent split time across all 5 intervals. This is aerobic base work - you should be able to hold a conversation. Focus on efficient stroke mechanics and breathing rhythm.

movements.name: Row
movements.notes: Steady sustainable pace, not max effort

movements.name: Jump Rope
movements.notes: Relaxed rhythm, avoid going to failure

movements.name: Pull-Up
movements.notes: Small sets (2-3 reps) to preserve shoulders and maintain aerobic pace

metcon.target_score: Target 8-10 rounds. Pace should be very consistent - this is long aerobic work, not sprint intervals.

movements.name: Row Easy
movements.notes: Very low effort, focus on deep breathing

session_why.text: This session prioritizes aerobic base development through sustained low-to-moderate intensity work. The 3-minute row intervals with 2-minute rest build aerobic capacity without entering the glycolytic system, while the 18-minute AMRAP maintains heart rate in the aerobic zone (65-75% max HR). This session represents the ~80% low-intensity volume recommended in polarized training models to support concurrent strength and endurance adaptation.
session_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
session_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movement_why.text: Rowing provides low-impact cardiovascular stress with full-body engagement. Single-unders add rhythmic coordination work without high impact, and pull-ups maintain pulling strength in small manageable sets. This combination develops cardiovascular endurance (oxygen delivery capacity) and stamina (energy processing and utilization) across Glassman's ten physical skills while avoiding excessive muscular fatigue.

loading_why.text: The 3-minute intervals with 2-minute rest provide a 3:2 work:rest ratio appropriate for aerobic development - sufficient recovery to maintain quality without full phosphocreatine restoration. The conversational pace prescription ensures athletes stay below anaerobic threshold. The AMRAP uses moderate volume per movement (15 cal, 60 singles, 8 pull-ups) to prevent local failure and maintain sustainable aerobic output for the full 18 minutes, aligning with oxidative system dominance beyond 10+ minutes of continuous work.

movements.name: Hip Circles
movements.notes: Hip mobility for cycling and squatting

movements.name: Front Squat
movements.notes: Heavy loading requires full recovery between sets. E3MOM format provides adequate phosphocreatine restoration. Focus on upright torso, elbows high, full depth. This is strength work - move with control and precision.

movements.name: Assault Bike
movements.notes: High intensity, push hard on each set

movements.name: Front Squat
movements.notes: Significantly lighter than strength block. Quick singles or small sets to manage fatigue.

metcon.target_score: Target time: 8-12 minutes. This is a glycolytic burn - expect high heart rate and leg fatigue. Pace the bike to leave gas for the squats.

movements.name: Assault Bike Easy
movements.notes: Very low effort, active recovery to clear lactate

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch
movements.notes: Release hip flexors from squat and bike volume

movements.name: Foam Roll - Quads
movements.notes: Address quad fatigue, slow deliberate passes

session_why.text: This session targets lactate threshold through a descending-rep scheme (21-15-9) that maintains high intensity across 8-12 minutes. The bike-squat couplet creates continuous lower body demand with incomplete recovery between sets, training the body's ability to buffer hydrogen ions and maintain power output under accumulating fatigue. The medium time domain ensures glycolytic system dominance while the descending reps create psychological momentum as fatigue increases.
session_why.url_or_ref: Ch. 3 - Energy Substrates and Metabolic Systems

movement_why.text: Front squats and assault bike both heavily tax the lower body, creating metabolic stress in the quadriceps and hip flexors. This intentional movement redundancy drives lactate accumulation and tests the athlete's ability to sustain power output when local muscular fatigue is high. The combination develops stamina (energy processing under fatigue) while maintaining the squat movement pattern under cardiovascular stress.

loading_why.text: The E3MOM format in the strength block provides full phosphocreatine recovery for heavy front squats at 80% 1RM, allowing quality neural adaptation without systemic fatigue. The metcon uses light load (95/65 lbs, approximately 40-50% of strength load) to allow fast cycling and prevent technical breakdown under fatigue. The descending rep scheme (21-15-9) manages volume while maintaining intensity - total of 45 reps per movement keeps time domain in the glycolytic window (8-12 min target).
